summary: Jeff Atwood announces improvements to his ASP.NET CAPTCHA server control, detailing a list of enhancements including better font sizing, improved warping algorithms, and removal of confusing characters like 1, 0, I, and O. He emphasizes the tradeoff between OCR-resistance and human readability, suggesting that most applications can use shorter, less warped CAPTCHAs since any CAPTCHA is usually sufficient to block casual bot attacks. The control is available for download from CodeProject.
key_points: The improved control now respects standard ASP.NET server control properties and adds configurable length and warping options
Confusing characters (1, 0, I, O) were removed to improve human readability
For most applications, having any CAPTCHA is sufficient to block casual bot attacks, so extreme distortion isn't necessary
The warping algorithm was improved to prevent drawing outside the bounding box
money_quotes: If you are willing to sacrifice less OCR-ability for more human readability, you can adjust the CaptchaLength and CaptchaFontWarping properties to taste.
For most applications, simply having a CAPTCHA of any sort is probably enough to block casual bot attacks, and shorter less warped phrases are definitely a lot easier to read.
key_insight: Most applications don't need extremely distorted CAPTCHAs—any CAPTCHA is usually enough to deter casual bots, so optimizing for human readability is a reasonable tradeoff.
